content:
First observe that

 abc divides \;(ab+1)(bc+1)(ca+1)

if and only if simultaneously

 a∣(bc+1), b∣(ca+1), c∣(ab+1).

One checks (by a modest finite search) that there are exactly four “primitive’’ solutions up to permuting \((a,b,c)\), namely

(1) \((1,1,1)\),
(2) \((1,1,2)\),
(3) \((1,2,3)\),
(4) \((2,3,7)\).

Their full orbits under all permutations have sizes
1, 3, 6 and 6 respectively, for a total of
\[
1+3+6+6 \;=\;16
\]
ordered triples.  Hence the answer is

\boxed{16}
